extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The study focuses on a simple yet representative thermal incubator system. The physical twin consists of an insulated styrofoam box equipped with a heatbed, a 0.02 m³/s fan for air circulation, and three DS18S20 high‑precision 1‑Wire temperature sensors (±0.5 °C accuracy from –10 °C to +85 °C). The box volume is 0.03 m³. A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B runs the controller and low‑level driver, communicating with the heatbed via MOSFETs isolated by optocouplers. The system operates in a laboratory environment for temperature regulation, making it suitable for digital‑twin experimentation due to its open‑source hardware, modularity, and well‑defined thermal dynamics.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin delivers real‑time monitoring, visualization, and control of the incubator. It provides a dashboard that streams sensor and controller data from a time‑series database, enabling operators to observe temperature trajectories. On‑demand calibration automatically runs the Model B parameter estimation (four‑parameter thermal model) to update the twin’s predictive accuracy. Anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation are performed with a Kalman filter that fuses measured temperatures with the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature. What‑if simulations are executed via co‑simulation, feeding historical or live PT data to evaluate controller parameter changes. The self‑adaptation loop automatically detects behavioral shifts, schedules experiments, recalibrates the model, reconfigures the Kalman filter, runs what‑if analyses, and updates the controller, thereby closing the loop between PT and DT.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin is implemented with a RabbitMQ message broker (running on the Raspberry Pi) for inter‑component communication. The controller is a bang‑bang logic implemented in Python on the Pi, while the low‑level driver abstracts sensor/actuator I/O. Thermal dynamics are modeled with two simulation models: Model A (two free parameters) and Model B (four free parameters). Calibration uses an automated fitting routine. A Kalman filter (implemented in Python) estimates the heat‑bed temperature. Co‑simulation is performed using a generic co‑simulation framework that consumes PT data streams. Data are stored in a time‑series database and visualized on a dashboard. All source code, hardware schematics, and datasets are released under an open‑source license at https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example-incubator. The PCB design uses MOSFETs and optocouplers to isolate the Pi from the 4 A heat‑bed current. The system leverages the DS18S20 1‑Wire sensors, Raspberry Pi GPIO, and the RabbitMQ server to enable real‑time data exchange and control.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin does not specify a fixed polling or update interval; the low‑level driver is described as periodically reading temperature data, but the exact frequency is not given. No explicit latency or synchronization requirements are mentioned. The only time‑related figure is the fan airflow mixing time of approximately 2 s (Document 6).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system comprises a single physical twin (the incubator) and a single digital twin instance that mirrors its state. The architecture is centralized: a RabbitMQ broker on the Raspberry Pi coordinates all components. The controller owns the control logic, the low‑level driver owns sensor/actuator communication, and the communication server owns message routing. No hierarchical or multiple twin instances are described. Coordination is achieved via AMQP messages; the controller publishes control commands and state updates, while the low‑level driver subscribes to commands and publishes sensor readings. Responsibilities: controller – compute control actions; low‑level driver – interface with PT hardware; communication server – broker messages.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital twin is orchestrated as a client‑server architecture with a RabbitMQ message broker at its core. The Raspberry Pi hosts three software components: (1) the controller, which implements a bang‑bang control logic with configurable limits (LL, UL), heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C); (2) the low‑level driver, which abstracts the 1‑Wire DS18S20 sensors and MOSFET‑controlled heatbed/fan actuators; and (3) the RabbitMQ communication server, which routes AMQP messages between the controller and driver. The PCB design decouples the high‑current heatbed driver from the Pi via MOSFETs and optocouplers, ensuring safe operation. Resource management is handled locally on the Pi; the twin does not rely on external compute resources. System‑wide coordination is achieved through the AMQP message flow: sensor readings → driver → broker → controller → broker → actuator commands.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The twin integrates with external systems only through the RabbitMQ broker, which can be deployed on‑premises or in the cloud. Data exchange follows the AMQP protocol; messages are serialized in JSON (implied by typical usage). No other interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) are mentioned. The twin can be extended to publish its state to external dashboards or time‑series databases (e.g., InfluxDB) as described in the future work section, but such integrations are not yet implemented. Therefore, horizontal integration is limited to the internal RabbitMQ broker and potential future extensions to external monitoring services.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Model A (two free parameters: C_air = 616.56 J/K, G_box = 0.65 J/K) and Model B (four free parameters: C_air = 486.12 J/K, G_box = 0.856 J/K, C_heater = 33.65 J/K, G_heater = 0.87 J/K) are the analytical behavioral models used in the incubator DT. Temperature data are collected from three sensors (T1, T2, T3) and the fan voltage (12 V or 18 V) and heatbed state are sent to a RabbitMQ message broker. A time‑series database stores the raw sensor streams and controller states for real‑time visualization. Calibration is performed automatically (on‑demand) by running the calibration experiment described in section 3.3, which generates a data trajectory used to fit the model parameters. Model B is used within a Kalman filter for anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation, while Model A provides a rough prediction baseline. The models are updated by calibration algorithms that adjust parameters to match observed data, and the updated models feed into simulation and optimisation algorithms.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators include a fan (operating at 12 V or 18 V) and a heatbed heater. Control logic is implemented by a bang‑bang controller with parameters: lower limit (LL), upper limit (UL), heating duration (H), and waiting duration (C). The controller receives temperature measurements and sends commands via RabbitMQ; the low‑level driver issues actuator commands and reports states back to RabbitMQ. Actuation ranges: fan voltage 12–18 V, heater on/off with timing defined by H and C. Safety constraints are enforced by a safe protocol in the low‑level driver, preventing unsafe temperature excursions. Addressing is handled through message topics on RabbitMQ, allowing remote control and monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Three temperature sensors are deployed: T1 measures ambient room temperature outside the box, T2 is positioned at the hottest point inside the box, and T3 at the coldest point. Sensors are read periodically by the low‑level driver, which uploads the data to RabbitMQ. Spatial distribution places T1 outside, T2 and T3 inside at distinct locations to capture temperature gradients. Sampling frequency and data transmission rates are not explicitly specified, but the low‑level driver reads temperature data at a regular interval and transmits it via RabbitMQ to the controller and data store. Accuracy and precision specifications are not provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured through an on‑demand calibration process (section 3.3) that fits Model A and Model B parameters to measured temperature trajectories. Validation is performed by comparing model predictions to sensor data; discrepancies are quantified (e.g., maximum temperature difference of 7 °C with 12 V fan, reduced to 6 °C with 18 V fan). Uncertainty sources include sensor placement, temperature gradients, and model simplifications. Error detection and correction are handled by a Kalman filter that performs anomaly detection and estimates the unmeasured heatbed temperature. Quality assurance includes real‑time monitoring, automated calibration, co‑simulation for what‑if analysis, and a self‑adaptation loop that schedules experiments, recalibrates models, and reconfigures controllers based on detected changes in plant behavior.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data from three DS18S20 1‑Wire sensors is read by the low‑level driver on a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B. The driver periodically uploads the readings to a RabbitMQ server (AMQP 0‑9‑1) that serves as the communication backbone for the digital twin. The data is transmitted as sensor messages over the local network (Wi‑Fi/Bluetooth) to the DT components.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The controller, running on the same Raspberry Pi, receives temperature measurements from RabbitMQ and sends control commands (fan on/off, heatbed on/off) back to the low‑level driver via RabbitMQ. The low‑level driver validates the commands using a safe protocol, executes the actuator actions, and publishes the resulting control states to RabbitMQ for monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The system uses RabbitMQ (AMQP 0‑9‑1) as the message broker, hosted on the Raspberry Pi 4. Communication between the controller, low‑level driver, and sensors occurs over the Pi’s Wi‑Fi/Bluetooth interfaces. The topology is a single‑node broker with local network connectivity; no explicit clustering, redundancy, or security layers are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: All DT software components (controller, low‑level driver, RabbitMQ server) are deployed on a single Raspberry Pi 4 Model B, an edge/on‑premise device. The Pi provides the necessary CPU, memory, and storage for the lightweight Python/Node.js processes and the RabbitMQ broker. No additional scalability or high‑availability mechanisms are specified.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The incubator DT lifecycle is articulated in several distinct phases:
1. **Conceptual & Physical Twin (PT) Design** – selection of hardware (Raspberry Pi 4, DS18S20 sensors, MOSFET‑based PCB, fan, heatbed, styrofoam enclosure) and construction of the physical incubator.
2. **Digital Twin (DT) Modeling** – development of two thermal models: Model A (2 free parameters) and Model B (4 free parameters). Model B is chosen for its superior prediction of heatbed inertia.
3. **Calibration & Validation** – experimental runs to fit Model B parameters; calibration is performed on‑demand via a dedicated calibration routine.
4. **Deployment & Runtime Integration** – installation of RabbitMQ as the communication broker, implementation of a low‑level driver (sensor/actuator abstraction), and a bang‑bang controller (LL, UL, H, C parameters) on the Raspberry Pi.
5. **Runtime Monitoring & Insight Generation** – real‑time visualization of PT status in a dashboard, anomaly detection, and heatbed state estimation using a Kalman filter that fuses Model B with sensor data.
6. **Self‑Adaptation Loop** – detection of plant behavior change triggers an experiment schedule, re‑calibration of Model B, re‑configuration of the Kalman filter and controller, and what‑if simulations to re‑optimize control parameters.
7. **Evolution & Governance** – iterative refinement of models and algorithms, with all code and data released as open source (GitHub repository). Transition management is handled by calibration updates that replace model parameters and by the Kalman filter’s state updates, ensuring the DT remains aligned with the PT.

Representation types evolve from a purely physical system to a hybrid PT/DT system comprising hardware, simulation models, data streams, and control algorithms, with governance implied by the open‑source release and iterative calibration cycle.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The engineering process follows a model‑based, data‑driven workflow:
- **Model Development** – two thermal models are coded (Model A and Model B) and validated against experimental temperature traces.
- **Calibration** – an automated calibration routine adjusts the free parameters of Model B to match measured data; this routine is invoked on‑demand.
- **Implementation** – a RabbitMQ server (running on the Pi) mediates message passing between the low‑level driver, controller, and DT components.
- **Control Logic** – a bang‑bang controller with parameters LL (lower limit), UL (upper limit), H (heating duration), and C (waiting duration) is implemented in Python on the Pi.
- **Data Acquisition** – DS18S20 sensors provide temperature readings; the low‑level driver uploads data to RabbitMQ.
- **Quality Assurance** – real‑time dashboards provide visual validation; anomaly detection via Kalman filtering offers automated checks; what‑if co‑simulations test controller parameter changes.
- **Evolution Management** – the self‑adaptation loop automatically schedules new experiments, re‑calibrates the model, and re‑configures the controller, enabling continuous improvement.
- **Version Control & Governance** – while explicit VCS details are not stated, the open‑source GitHub repository implies use of standard Git workflows for code and data versioning.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
Decision support is achieved through:
- **Real‑time Visualization** – a dashboard consumes RabbitMQ messages to plot PT status (temperature, heater/fan state) in real time.
- **Anomaly Detection & State Estimation** – a Kalman filter, driven by Model B, estimates the unmeasured heatbed temperature and flags deviations from expected behavior.
- **What‑if Simulations** – co‑simulation of the PT and DT using historical or live data allows exploration of controller parameter changes (LL, UL, H, C) to optimize performance.
- **Self‑Adaptation Decisions** – upon detecting a plant behavior change, the DT schedules an experiment, recalibrates Model B, updates the Kalman filter, runs what‑if simulations, and re‑configures the controller, closing the loop.
- **Analytics & Confidence** – the Kalman filter provides covariance estimates for state uncertainty; simulation results give confidence intervals for controller performance; dashboard alerts convey confidence levels to operators.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The low‑level driver incorporates a safe protocol to keep the device in a safe state. Communication between the controller and the low‑level driver is performed via RabbitMQ, a mature, lightweight, open‑source message broker. No explicit cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, fail‑safe mechanisms, risk assessment approaches, or safety‑critical system considerations are described in the documents.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. **2.1.3** **The Fan** We installed a fan (see fig. 3(c)) inside the box to circulate the air inside the box for making the temperature as uniformly distributed as possible. The fan airflow is about 0 _._ 02m [3] s _[−]_ [1] . With a box volume of 0 _._ 03m [3], the fan should take 2 seconds to move all the air within the box. **2.1.4** **The Raspberry Pi** A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (see fig. 3(d)) was selected to implement the controller. The General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) connectors on the Raspberry Pi can be used to connect many accessories such as temperature sensors, fan, and the heatbed. Furthermore it supports wireless internet out of the box, with built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, 5 which simplifies deployment of the controller. **2.1.5** **The Temperature Sensor** It is necessary to get feedback from the temperature sensors for feedback control. To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C from -10 to +85 °C, which is suitable for the physical incubator. And each DS18S20 has a unique 64-bit serial code, which allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus. Such 1-wire bus support to connect multiple sensors with only one GPIO. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi. Instead, MOSFETs are used to separate the control signals from the Raspberry Pi and the driving currents. In order to decouple the control signals from the Raspberry Pi to the MOSFETs, we use optocouplers. The PCB schematic is shown in fig. 4. **Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

The General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) connectors on the Raspberry Pi can be used to connect many accessories such as temperature sensors, fan, and the heatbed. Furthermore it supports wireless internet out of the box, with built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, 5 which simplifies deployment of the controller. **2.1.5** **The Temperature Sensor** It is necessary to get feedback from the temperature sensors for feedback control. To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C from -10 to +85 °C, which is suitable for the physical incubator. And each DS18S20 has a unique 64-bit serial code, which allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus. Such 1-wire bus support to connect multiple sensors with only one GPIO. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi. Instead, MOSFETs are used to separate the control signals from the Raspberry Pi and the driving currents. In order to decouple the control signals from the Raspberry Pi to the MOSFETs, we use optocouplers. The PCB schematic is shown in fig. 4. DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

processes [1].

directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:

**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and

more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
